corrected_thesis: The 'learn from data despite noise' stack. Base-rates anchor priors; Bayesian updating adjusts beliefs as evidence arrives; law-of-large-numbers calibrates when sample sizes are sufficient to trust; scientific method structures the evidence-gathering process; statistical-learning-theory formalizes hypothesis selection from data; resilience sustains the process through contradictory results, failed experiments, and ambiguous signals. This stack differs from Quantitative Calibration (which shares 5 of 6 members) by swapping systems-thinking for resilience — shifting emphasis from 'how probabilistic outcomes cascade through systems' to 'how to keep updating beliefs when the evidence fights back'.
what_this_stack_defeats: Giving up after disconfirming evidence, overfitting to noise, abandoning priors too quickly or too slowly
notes: Auto-named cluster — no pre-existing thesis. Near-duplicate of Quantitative Calibration (shares base-rates, bayesian, LLN, scientific-method, statistical-learning-theory). Divergence member: resilience replaces systems-thinking. The resilience article confirms this angle: 'optimistic explanation style' sustains researchers through repeated failures; 'what would you have to believe' analysis forces articulation of assumptions under stress. This family captures the psychological durability dimension of quantitative reasoning that Quantitative Calibration misses. Whether this warrants a separate family at runtime or should be merged is an open design question.
